thread.title: COP29 live updates: U.N. climate deal, $300 billion for poor nations, sparks backlash
thread.title_full: COP29 live updates: U.N. climate deal, $300 billion for poor nations, sparks backlash

author: Chico Harlan
title: COP29 live updates: U.N. climate deal, $300 billion for poor nations, sparks backlash
text:
BAKU, Azerbaijan — More than a day past the scheduled end of the [U.N. Climate Change Conference](https://www.washingtonpost.com/climate-environment/2024/11/22/cop29-climate-deal-funding-baku/?itid=lk_inline_manual_1), negotiators from almost 200 nations approved a deal to marshal the vast sums of money that poorer nations need to cope with [global warming’s worst effects. ](https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/2024/05/31/india-heat-deaths/?itid=lk_inline_manual_1)
The COP29 conference venue is finally emptying out
[Return to menu](#secondary-nav)
BAKU, Azerbaijan — Empty pavilions, cleaned-out snack cases and closed first-aid stations have replaced the once-bustling atmosphere that characterized the COP29 conference venue, a windowless soccer stadium on the outskirts of Baku.
Indian delegate slams finance deal as ‘optical illusion’
[Return to menu](#secondary-nav)
Chandni Raina, an adviser to India’s Finance Ministry, voiced outrage at the way the climate finance deal was muscled through over her country’s objections. She alleged that the COP29 presidency “stage-managed” the adoption of the deal, saying India was not allowed to publicly air its concerns beforehand.
“We are very unhappy, disappointed with the process, and object to the adoption of this agenda,” Raina said to applause. “This document is nothing more than an optical illusion.”
[Return to menu](#secondary-nav)
COP29 President Mukhtar Babayev just gaveled through the final deal on climate finance, drawing loud and sustained applause in the meeting hall at 2:40 a.m. local time here.
The final deal is out. It calls for $300 billion in climate aid.
[Return to menu](#secondary-nav)
International negotiators at the annual climate summit Saturday night reached a deal that would ramp up financial assistance to relatively poor nations to about $300 billion a year over the next decade.
The COP29 presidency, which oversees the talks, publicly posted the deal to a [U.N. website](https://unfccc.int/documents) after privately circulating it among negotiators.
[Return to menu](#secondary-nav)
COP29 President Mukhtar Babayev just told delegates gathered in the massive meeting hall: “We need 20 to 25 minutes to finalize the documents and [then] we will start the discussions.”
Babayev appeared to be referring to the final deal on climate finance that everyone has been anxiously awaiting. We seem to be in the home stretch of the talks now.
[Return to menu](#secondary-nav)
In what could be the final hours of these talks, nearly all delegates have assembled in a massive meeting hall. The scenes of last-minute negotiations are right out of a movie: small circles of people huddled around computers and leaning over tables. There is also some hand-shaking. Spirits are higher than they were several hours ago.
China looks to step into global vacuum as Trump pulls the U.S. back
[Return to menu](#secondary-nav)
BAKU, Azerbaijan — When climate negotiators from around the world began meeting here for the U.N. talks known as COP29, less than a week after climate-change skeptic Donald Trump was elected president again, one country in particular was ready to step into the U.S.-size gap.
China, the world’s renewable-energy leader and its biggest emitter of planet-warming greenhouse gases, is presenting itself as fully committed to the fight against climate change.
This is an excerpt from a full story.
